data.title: Signs of trouble in the relief rally
data.description:
SIGNS OF TROUBLE IN THE RELIEF RALLY

A look at the day ahead in European and global markets from Rae Wee

Asian stocks and European stock futures surged on Thursday, joining the relief rally over a 90-day reprieve on most new U.S. tariffs, but that policy reversal excluded China, where an intensifying trade war signalled more trouble ahead for markets.

Indeed, the dollar struggled to hold overnight gains, particularly against the safe-haven yen and Swiss franc, and U.S. stock futures turned lower after a brief early rally, a potential warning of wavering investor confidence in the U.S. economy.

As economists at J.P. Morgan put it, this is "merely the end of the beginning", and the U.S. war on trade is far from over.

Wall Street indexes logged their largest daily percentage gains in more than a decade overnight, relieved that, with U.S. President Donald Trump's latest U-turn on tariffs, global trade was no longer about to grind to a complete halt.

But the gloves have come off between the world's two trade powerhouses, with Trump dealing an explicit blow to China by raising the levy on Chinese imports to 125%, up from the 104% duty that came into effect on Wednesday.

China had raised additional duties on American products to 84% and imposed restrictions on 18 U.S. companies.

By contrast, Vietnam - a huge beneficiary of global manufacturers' "China plus one" strategy that aims to diversify supply chains - said on Thursday it had come to an agreement with the United States to launch negotiations for a trade pact.

Chinese stocks opened on a strong note on Thursday and Hong Kong's Hang Seng Index surged 4%, even as the onshore yuan tumbled to its weakest level in more than 17 years.

Some analysts attributed the rise to hopes for talks between the world's two largest economies, as well as support from Beijing for the markets and the economy.

A manic bond selloff also stabilised on Thursday as U.S. Treasury yields retreated, after heavy selling in the previous sessions had reignited fears of fragility in the world's biggest bond market.

In other news, the March report on U.S. inflation is due later on Thursday, although it will likely be of less importance to markets given the figures mostly predate the latest round of hefty levies imposed by Trump.

Still, an upside surprise could be nasty, given it would be just a taste of the sharp price increases coming once tariffs fully kick in.

Key developments that could influence markets on Thursday:

- Tariff news

- U.S. inflation data (March)

Trying to keep up with the latest tariff news?

data.title: Biocon Arm Gets US FDA Nod for Oncology Drug Jobevne
data.description:
Biocon Biologics, a subsidiary of Biocon , has received approval from the US Food and Drug Administration (US FDA) for its Jobevne (bevacizumab-nwgd), a biosimilar Bevacizumab for intravenous use, according to a Thursday filing to the Indian stock exchanges.

JOBEVNE is an antibody used to treat several different types of cancer. It is a biosimilar to the reference product Avastin (bevacizumab).

data.title: Sharjah Cement and Industrial Development to Halt Trading in Abu Dhabi
data.description:
Sharjah Cement and Industrial Development (ADX:SCIDC) will temporarily halt trading on the Abu Dhabi Securities Exchange from 11 am local time on Thursday due to its annual general meeting.

The Abu Dhabi-listed cement manufacturer will resume trading, pending the publication of its meeting results, according to a Thursday release.

data.title: Asset Plus' Munroe Lane Loses About NZ$9.2 Million in Valuation
data.description:
Asset Plus said its property at Munroe Lane has been independently assessed and reduced in value from NZ$116.2 million to NZ$107.0 million, according to a Thursday New Zealand bourse filing.

The impact of the valuation movement represents a forecast AU$0.025 per share reduction in the net tangible assets (NTA) to AU$0.323 per share and reflects the softening of the office leasing market, according to the filing.

The independent valuation, forecast valuation movements, and forecast reduction in NTA remain subject to external audit and will be confirmed in the company's consolidated financial statements slated for May 27, the filing added.

The company's shares were up 2% in Thursday's close.

data.title: CMGE Technology Names Co-CEO; Shares Rise 3%
data.description:
CMGE Technology Group named Wang Chunye as co-chief executive officer at the mobile game publisher effective Wednesday, according to a same-day filing with the Hong Kong bourse.

Wang is the general manager of Lemando Interactive Entertainment (Shanghai). He has worked with companies associated with NetDragon and Giant Network Group , the filing said.

Xiao Jian remains as chairman and CEO, the filing said.

Shares in CMGE jumped 3% during Thursday's afternoon trading.

data.title: Skanska Bags GBP43 Million Rail Bridge Replacement Contract in UK
data.description:
Skanska (SKA-B.ST) secured a 43 million-pound-sterling contract from British railway infrastructure manager Network Rail to replace the Clifton rail bridge in England.

The contract includes demolition and replacement of the existing structure with a new 130-meter single-span steel structure, the Swedish construction company said Thursday.

The project's construction has begun and is planned to be completed in March 2026.

data.title: Fakeeh Care Plans Share Buyback Program
data.description:
Dr. Soliman Abdel Kader Fakeeh Hospital Co. (SASE:4017), d/b/a Fakeeh Care, plans to launch a share buyback program of up to 1,038,000 shares.

The healthcare company intends to hold the repurchased shares in the treasury, according to a Thursday filing. The repurchase requires approval from the company's extraordinary general assembly.

data.title: Tryptamine, Swinburne University Agree to Start Trial for Binge Eating Disorder Treatment Drug Candidate; Shares Rise 10%
data.description:
Tryptamine Therapeutics and Swinburne University have entered an agreement to start a clinical trial to assess the safety and efficacy of TRP-8803, a psilocin-based drug candidate for the treatment of Binge Eating Disorder (BED) along with psychotherapy, according to a Thursday filing with the Australian bourse.

The trial aims to assess the drug's safety, with secondary goals of evaluating its impact on binge-eating episodes and weight-related indicators in BED patients, with results expected by the fourth quarter of the year, the filing said.

Shares of the company rose 10% in recent Thursday trade.

data.title: China's yuan hits post financial crisis low as trade war ramps up
data.description:
Key points:
  • Yuan weakens to lowest level since 2007
  • Central bank lowers fixing for sixth session
  • Bank opting for modest, gradual yuan depreciation
  • Yuan weakness comes as Sino-US trade tension intensifies

China's yuan hit its lowest against the dollar since the global financial crisis on Thursday, with the central bank cutting guidance for the sixth successive trading session amid an intensifying Sino-U.S. trade war.

Beijing has imposed steep tariffs on U.S. imports in response to similar U.S. action. Though U.S. President Donald Trump said he would temporarily lower duties recently imposed on dozens of countries, he increased those on Chinese goods.

"The U.S. and China are currently in a powerplay game of brinkmanship," said ING global head of markets Chris Turner.

"Until a deal is announced or a big, bilateral meeting confirmed, USD/CNY will now be the focal attention of the FX market."

A weaker yuan would make Chinese exports cheaper and alleviate tariff impact on the economy. However, a sharp decline could also increase unwanted capital outflow pressure and risk financial stability, analysts and economists said.

The central bank will not allow sharp yuan declines and has instructed major state-owned lenders to reduce dollar purchases, people with knowledge of the matter told Reuters.

The onshore yuan slipped to 7.3518 a dollar in early trade, its weakest since December 26, 2007. It pared intraday losses and traded 0.02% higher at 7.3428 as of 0516 GMT, but was still down about 1.2% this month.

Its offshore counterpart was at 7.3558 at 0516 GMT, down 0.14%. It hit an all-time low of 7.4288 on Tuesday.

Prior to market open, the People's Bank of China set the midpoint - around which it allows the yuan to trade in a 2% band - at 7.2092, the weakest since September 11, 2023. That compared with the 7.3484 Reuters estimate .

The central bank has been lowering the midpoint at a measured pace, with Thursday's cut contributing to the day's decline, traders said.

The PBOC loosened its grip on the yuan this week by allowing the currency to weaken past 7.2. Still, its guidance is stronger than market projections in what traders and analysts interpreted as an attempt to keep the yuan steady.

The steadily weaker guidance dragged down its value against major trading partners. The CFETS yuan basket index (.CFSCNYI), a gauge that measures the yuan against a basket of currencies, fell to 98.18 on Thursday, the lowest since September 2024, according to Reuters calculations based on official data.

The bank is focusing on a steady yuan even as the trade war challenges the competitiveness of China's export sector, indicating that stability remains the priority.

"A modest, gradual depreciation of the yuan is still the preference," Societe Generale economists said in a client note.

China will only allow gradual depreciation as stability matters for confidence in Chinese assets, and the tariffs are "just too big to be offset by FX depreciation," they said.

Separately, China and Hong Kong shares rose on Thursday. The Hong Kong dollar hovered near a four-year high against the dollar on persistent inflows through the southbound leg of the stock trading link. It last traded at 7.7616 as of 0516 GMT.

Mainland investors purchased more than HK$35 billion ($4.51 billion) worth of Hong Kong stocks on Wednesday, the highest on record.

Marco Sun, chief financial market analyst at MUFG Bank, said a strong Hong Kong dollar was critical for the financial hub during times of heightened financial market volatility.

"And the renminbi is likely to enter a period of orderly depreciation," he said.

($1 = 7.7616 Hong Kong dollars)

data.title: U.S. Recession Looks Less Likely After Trump's Tariff PauseMarket Talk
data.description: Goldman Sachs analysts revert to their previous non-recession baseline forecast for the U.S. GDP growth, expectating it at 0.5% with a 45% probability of a recession, they say in a note. U.S. President Trump has announced a 90-day pause on certain tariffs to most countries. Before Trump's announcement, Goldman Sachs analysts had shifted to a recession baseline in response to the additional country-specific tariffs that went into effect Wednesday morning. As a result of the pause, "we are now reverting to our previous non-recession baseline forecast, which projected 2025 Q4/Q4 GDP growth of 0.5%, a peak core PCE inflation rate of 3.5%, a 45% probability of recession, and three consecutive 25bp 'insurance' cuts by the Federal Reserve in June, July, and September," the analysts say. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

data.title: Cheffelo issues trading update for Q1 2025
data.description:
Cheffelo, a leading, profitable Scandinavian mealkit provider that helps people eat well without the hassle of planning and shopping, today issues a trading update for the first quarter of 2025. Full results for the period are scheduled to be announced on 7 May 2025.

Net sales for Q1 2025 amounted to MSEK 336.3 (288.7 Q1 2024), an increase of 16.5% versus last year. Adjusted for currency effects, that equals a growth of 17.6%.

The timing of Easter moving from Q1 in 2024 to Q2 in 2025 affects the comparability of Net Sales and other metrics. Excluding the affected Easter week, the growth was 14.0% adjusted for currency effects.

Active customers for the first quarter were 86.8 thousand (81.5 Q1 2024), an increase of 6.5% versus last year.

Cheffelo will invite investors, analysts and the media to a conference call and webcast with Q&A, which will take place on the day the Q1 results are published.

data.title: Japan to Keep Pushing for Tariff Relief After US Concessions
data.description:
Japan will continue pressing the US to review its tariff measures, hoping for relief after President Trump paused some reciprocal tariffs, said Economic Revitalisation Minister Ryosei Akazawa.

Tokyo, still facing tariffs on cars, auto parts, steel, and aluminum, sees the pause as a positive step and expects to be prioritized in upcoming trade talks.

US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent plans to meet officials from Japan, Vietnam, India, and South Korea soon.

Akazawa, recently appointed to lead the negotiations, emphasized Japan's intent to seek further tariff reductions.

data.title: European Markets Head Higher on US Tariff Relief
data.description:
European equity markets were poised for a strong open on Thursday, buoyed by news that U.S. President Donald Trump will reduce new tariff rates to 10% for most U.S. trade partners over a 90-day period to allow for negotiations.

The move marks a significant shift from the previously imposed 20% tariff on European Union imports.

However, uncertainty remains over whether the EU will be included in the temporary tariff reprieve, as the bloc recently announced its first round of countermeasures in response to U.S. trade actions.

In premarket trading, futures for the Euro Stoxx 50 and Stoxx 600 surged approximately 8% and 7%, respectively, signaling a strong rebound in investor sentiment across the region.

data.title: China Sanjiang Fine Chemicals' Management Contract Falls Through
data.description:
China Sanjiang Fine Chemicals has terminated an agreement to procure management services from Grand Novel Developments, a Wednesday filing with the Hong Kong bourse said.

The move came about as there is uncertainty regarding the logistic arrangement in the procurement of ethane, propane, butane, methanol, and silver among others amid tariffs imposed by the US.

The parties had entered into the management services contract in January, according to the chemicals company.

data.title: Ades Holding Renews Offshore Drilling Services Contract with Saudi Aramco
data.description:
Ades Holding Co. (SASE:2382) said Thursday that the 10-year offshore drilling services contract renewal announced Tuesday was signed with Saudi Arabian Oil Co. (SASE:2222), d/b/a Saudi Aramco.

The drilling services company's renewed deal offers a total estimated backlog of 1.09 billion Saudi riyals for an offshore jackup rig.
